critique.summary: The video titled 'It Took Me 30 Years to Solve This VFX Problem – Green Screen Problem' by Corridor Crew discusses longstanding challenges in visual effects related to green screen compositing. It explores the transparency and chroma key problems and presents a new approach involving neural networks and procedural generation to improve green screen effects.
mainPoints: Green screens have inherent issues that have made perfect compositing difficult for decades.
Traditional methods to solve transparency and chroma key problems have limitations.
The video proposes using neural networks and procedural generation as a novel solution to these problems.
The presenter shares their personal journey and experimentation over 30 years to address these VFX challenges.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about the difficulties in green screen compositing and the limitations of traditional chroma keying are well-known in the VFX industry and accurately represented. The use of neural networks and procedural generation as emerging techniques to improve compositing aligns with current trends in visual effects technology. However, the video is a single source from a popular YouTube channel and does not cite multiple independent expert sources or studies to fully validate the effectiveness of the proposed solution.
considerations: The video does not provide independent verification or peer-reviewed evidence supporting the new method's superiority over existing techniques.
It is unclear how widely applicable or scalable the proposed neural network approach is across different production environments.
The video focuses on a personal narrative and demonstration rather than a comprehensive technical analysis or comparison with industry standards.
Potential limitations or challenges in implementing the new approach in commercial VFX pipelines are not discussed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the challenges and innovations in green screen keying technology, including the use of synthetic data and AI models. Commenters share insights on production difficulties, proprietary techniques, hardware limitations, and the impact of budget constraints on post-production quality.

comments.author: dylan604
comments.text:
The sad thing about this is the problems encountered during post from the production team saying "fix it post" during the shoot. I've been on set for green screen shoots where the lighting was not done properly. I watched the gaffer walk across the set taking readings from his meter before saying the lighting was good. I flip on the waveform and told him it was not even (which never goes down well when camera dept tells the gaffer it's not right). He put up an argument, went back and took measurements again before repeating it was good. I flipped the screen around and showed him where it was obviously not even. A third set of meter readings and he starts adjust lights. Once the footage was in post, the fx team commented about how easy the keys were because of the even lighting.

The problem is that the vast majority of people on set have no clue what is going on in post. To the point, when the budget is big enough, a post supervisor is present on production days to give input so "fixing it in post" is minimized. When there is no budget, you'll see situations just like in the first 30 seconds of TFA's video. A single lamp lighting the background so you can easily see the light falling off and the shadows from wrinkles where the screen was just pulled out of the bag 10 minutes before shooting. People just don't realize how much light a green screen takes. They also fail to have enough space so they can pull the talent far enough off the wall to avoid the green reflecting back onto the talent's skin.

TL;DR They solved something to make post less expensive because they cut corners during production.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed and realistic account of on-set challenges with green screen lighting and how it affects post-production, reflecting common industry knowledge.

comments.author: Springtime
comments.text: In an earlier video they made a couple years back about Disney's sodium vapor technique Paul Debevec suggested he was considering creating a dataset using a similar premise: filming enough perfectly masked references to be able to train models to achieve better keying. So it was interesting seeing Corridor tackle this by instead using synthetic data.
comments.analysis: This comment adds valuable context about related work in the field and the use of datasets for training models, showing awareness of ongoing research and techniques.

comments.author: diacritical
comments.text:
From ~04:10 till 05:00 they talk about sodium-vapor lights and how Disney has the exclusive rights to use it. From what I read the knowledge on how to make them is a trade secret, so it's not patented. Seems weird that it would be hard to recreate something from the 1950's.

I also wonder how many hours were wasted by people who had to use inferior technology because Disney kept it secret. Cutting out animals and objects from the background 1 frame at a time seems so mindnumbingly boring.
comments.analysis: The comment raises a valid point about trade secrets and the impact of proprietary technology on the industry, though some speculation is involved.

comments.author: vsviridov
comments.text: The community has managed to drastically lower hardware requirements, but so far I think only Nvidia cards are supported, so as an AMD owner I'm still missing out :(
comments.analysis: This is a relevant observation about hardware compatibility issues affecting accessibility, consistent with known trends in AI and graphics software.

comments.author: amelius
comments.text: There's still a bug: the glass with water does not distort the checker pattern in the background at 24:12.
comments.analysis: The comment points out a specific technical flaw in the video demonstration, showing attention to detail and constructive criticism.

comments.author: ralusek
comments.text: I'm a software engineer that, like the vast majority of you, uses AI/agents in my workflow every day. That being said, I have to admit that it feels a little weird to hear someone who does not write code say that they built something, without even mentioning that they had an agent build it (unless I missed that).
comments.analysis: This comment reflects on the perception of AI-assisted work and authorship, offering a subjective but relevant perspective on modern software development.

comments.author: superjan
comments.text: Watched this a few days ago. The video is light on technical details, except maybe that they used CGI to generate training data.
comments.analysis: A brief but accurate observation about the video's content, noting the lack of deep technical explanation.

comments.author: IshKebab
comments.text:
Pretty impressive results! Seems like someone has even made a GUI for it: https://github.com/edenaion/EZ-CorridorKey

Still Python unfortunately.
comments.analysis: This comment provides useful information about available tools and implementation details, though the 'unfortunately' remark is subjective.

comments.author: Computer0
comments.text: Looking forward to trying it out, 8gb of vram or unified memory required!
comments.analysis: A straightforward comment about hardware requirements, likely accurate but limited in depth.

comments.author: comex
comments.text:
See also this video comparing Corridor Key to traditional keyers:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=abNygtFqYR8
comments.analysis: Provides a helpful resource link for further comparison, adding value to the discussion.
